sections.title: Life of a retired Chairman
sections.content: Martin Baxter enjoying a relaxing flight at Aglou Beach in Morocco.

sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Tam (AKA Glorious Leader) reports

As Simon reports later in this edition, the club has responded to the Snaizeholme (Dodd Fell) ACP.  We have tried to be constructive and find an approach that will give us maximum use of Dodd Fell whilst allowing Menapia (the drone company) to carry out their research. The workings of the mitigation strategy are yet to be agreed, but it will be a clear rules based system and the flying status of the site will be clear. Hopefully there will be more information available in the next month. Our thanks are due to both Simon Hulme and Rob Urselmann for their work on this.

It’s March 30th as I write and already 5 Dales pilots have had flights over 100km. 3 of these were from the Long Mynd on 14th March, and 2 from Dodd on 26th (see Chris Fountain’s report later).  I guess the season has started then. We had some spectacular first years of XCs last year with James Maxwell flying 54km on his first XC - amazingly this has already been surpassed this year - Simon Hulme (yes him again) was one of the 100km flights from the Mynd, with 117km! Yes, his first XC! The mind boggles. Don’t you people realise how difficult it is to fly long distances in the UK! Well done Simon, an extraordinary achievement. Lets hope this early success in the season builds into a brill year for everyone.

I am somewhat distraught to also have to report some very sad news. Many in the club would know Geoff Yeadon who sadly passed away unexpectedly on March 29. Geoff had only last year decided to finish flying. A more unassuming and humble man it would be impossible to meet. Always a delight to meet him and share a laugh on the hill or in the pub afterwards - if you didn’t know you would likely not have guessed at his standing in the caving world. A global legend, world record holder  and cave diving pioneer with his partner in crime Geoff Crossley - they still hold the national record for the longest dives ever undertaken for both time and distance. He had a story or two, and more, to tell and they were worth listening to. His report on “The dead man’s handshake” cave dive was included in Chris Bonnington’s “Quest for Adventure”, a collection of stories about mankind’s great adventures - walking on the moon, climbing Everest etc. And included in them, cave diving in the Yorkshire Dales as related by Geoff. Just googling “youtube Geoff Yeadon” will give you an idea of his escapades. RIP Geoff, it was an honour to know you.

Messrs Yeadon and Crossley

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

Next time you have a spare thirty seconds or so in the air. Spend it looking up at the wing. No, this isn’t an April fools, do make sure you’re not going to be hitting anything in that time. Quite rightly we’re trained to be looking out and around for obvious reasons but we’re also told to be active pilots. How then do we know that our active piloting inputs are having the desired effect?

That effect, by the way, is keeping the glider both pitch and roll stable. I.e. allowing it to cut into lift, stopping dives and then accelerating through sink and keeping both halves of the wing pressured. Turbulence in air that we feel is on the scale from a few metres wide upward. Flying at 10m/s you’ll pass through that small scale in half a second or less. That means you have to be fairly fast in reaction in order to catch the bumps.

This month’s subject has been motivated by a few pilots who’ve been in contact about glider control and collapse avoidance. I’d been thinking about this a few years ago and put together a little camera to get footage of the glider and my hand positions. That’s the photo above. I thought it would be an interesting exercise to capture active piloting and reactions to collapses. I only had one uncaught collapse on the XAlps and that was before I put the camera together.

So back to looking at the wing. Ask yourself the question, are my inputs having the desired effect? Am I over or under controlling pitch and roll (both hands and hips remember)? Do I have sufficient contact on the brakes or rear riser to feel and react to what’s around me? By contact, I mean that first few cm where you have quite a bit of speed control. You can speed up as you pitch back as well as slow the glider if it pitches forward.

This matters because it promotes you having control of the situation in the short term over seconds as well as making decisions about what air to put yourself in over the longer term. You also save a few cm of height on every bump. That will add up as it becomes second nature.

So this month’s advice is go play in the spring air. Learn feedback and fine tune your reactions to some bouncy and fun conditions.

Also congratulations to Jonathan Aylward who gets his Pilot Rating this month. Well done!

sections.title: Dodd Fell March Epic
sections.content:
Chris Fountain reports

Spotted a good forecast a few days out but looked like the wind was always going to be a deciding factor.  It was top end for sure but having missed a good day at the Long Mynd two weeks previously because I thought the wind looked too strong that day I wasn't going to be caught out twice.

James Goldsborough  and I started making plans for an early start to try beat the wind and arranged to meet Rob C and Pete M at 10.30 on Cam High road to share cars.

I was surprised how few were out.  I'd heard a few very poor excuses as to why people couldn't make it. But the best one I heard was that he was going line dancing in the afternoon. I won't tell you who that was,  but I now have some serious concerns about his priorities in life. I will be having a stern word with him very soon.

Anyway, we arrived at the hill to find a few others already ground handling.  Excellent, not blown out yet then.

The wind was fresh for sure but nothing unmanageable.

Always best to not hang around too much if it’s already flyable, so started unpacking,  agreed on the task for the day, checked comms and got clipped in.

Simon and someone else had taken off at this point and it still looked ok.  Not much lift but everyone appeared to be going in the direction they wanted to go in.

Chris’s flight https://xcleague.com/xc/flights/2026404.html

I took off, turned right and flew along the main face of Dodd Fell.  Weak climbs coming off the rocks so hung around there and climbed up a few 100m.

The rest of the lads took off and flew over to join me.  We had decided not to hang around too long if it was working which it clearly was.  11.45 and 3 of us left the hill at approx. 4000ft followed by another 4 pilots a little lower but climbing nicely by the look of it.

I opted for the usual route out of Dodd.  The wind was WNW as predicted so headed along the front line of ridges down Hubberholme towards Buckden.

Not many clouds at this stage, just a few hard to see small puffs of cloud hidden by the approaching top cover.  I found a weak climb on the way to Buckden Pike that got me up enough to push on.  Rob pushed straight on to BP and connected with a nice climb running up the gully.  I went to join him but found a better climb just out front.  We both climbed a few 100m before the climbs joined up and we both circled up to the base of a nicely forming cloud.

First decision..  Over the moor!!.  It was a long glide with nothing really obvious to head for. I wasn't sure, so tucked in, hit the bar and glided for all I was worth.

I saw the smallest cloud just starting to form right in the middle of the moor so headed for that.  It didn't work. I ended up low on a very shallow ridge in the middle of nowhere. Luckily the makings of a very weak thermal started to drift my way.  I started climbing and Rob came to join me from the now very nice cloud over Buckden Pike.

Rob’s flight https://xcleague.com/xc/flights/2026380.html

Butty, Rob and I glided on over the moor working the weak lift.   It really started picking up around Brimham rocks where the thermals gained a lot more momentum.  4m climbs and nice clouds at the top.  Happy days. Still not much sunshine though.

The ground dropped away as we left the hills for the flats and flew down past Menwith Hill towards Harrogate.  Base now at about 5000ft and very cold. The forecast said minus7 at base.  Don't know if that was true but I was glad of my new heated gloves.

Leeds/Bradford airspace was now coming into play.  I told Butty to stay left of Menwith Hill and he would be fine, so we all followed a direct line parallel to the edge of airspace until it turned a corner and gave us a bit more room to play with.

The next issue was the Sherburn ATZ.  The better looking sky was to the east,  but my goal of Gainsborough was more to the west.  I stuck to my plan and pushed on towards my goal into a blue hole.  The sun was coming out now but never quite where we were, always just ahead of us.  The climbs were still there even under a patchy sky.

Made it to the next set of clouds quite easily with a rather buoyant glide only to realise we had found a patchy convergency line.  Well I call it a line, it was more a random series of clouds that looked just like any other clouds.  It was Rob that pointed out it was probably convergence.

Butty’s flight https://xcleague.com/xc/flights/2026371.html

Things got a bit scrappy after that.  Our comms were shocking and it turned out we had different goals too.  Butty and Rob had Boston as a goal while I had Gainsborough.  Both were more or less in the same direction anyway but with the predicted NW wind being more WNW and being forced to fly the wrong side of Sandtoft ATZ it made reaching my goal almost impossible.  I was never going to be able to get the required 22km back crosswind to my goal.  So I gave up.  I think Rob and Butty still had a chance as their goal was a lot further giving them more time to get back across.

So I decided to try to make up some KM's to make up for the lost multiplier Tesco points and pushed on crossing the Humber.  yes it was only the 5m wide bit.

The 3 of us were quite split up at this stage and I think all of us not knowing quite what the other was planning.

I decided to go land at Scunthorp (Mordor as it looks from the air) for easy access to the train station.  I headed over that way and spotted a sailplane climbing rather well.  I couldn't resist joining him above and showing him how to turn tightly and climb quickly.  It made me smile but I doubt he even noticed I was there.

So, another change of plan now I was back at 5000ft.  I headed over to Rob to fly with him.  Butty was just south of us heading towards Grimsby.  I assumed he had given up on his goal now.

I joined Rob for a couple of turns then headed off towards Grimsby to land and warm up.  I landed at Caister just outside Grimsby.

I thought Rob was following but he pushed on to Market Rasen and the train station there.  If only my radio worked I think we could have made better progress at the end and had a better retrieve together.

I landed,  warmed up and called an Uber to Market Rasen.  It didn't turn up so I got a bus to Grimsby instead to try to catch up with Butty. Unfortunately he managed to get the earlier train so I had an hour to wait for the next train to Doncaster,  then on to Leeds and home by 8.30.

James had managed to retrieve my car from Dodd and driven it back to my house so no need for a trip back up to collect any cars.  Thanks James.

No photos, I'm afraid.  Way too cold for any of that faffing!

sections.title: Dodd Fell TDA
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann & Simon Hulme report

As reported in last month’s SkyWords, there's an Airspace Change Proposal in progress with the CAA to fly a Beyond Visual Line Of Sight (BVLOS) drone at Dodd Fell this summer which covers the key part of the Dodd ridge line and the majority of the valley below.

Area of the TDA overlaid on the PG soaring heatmap from thermal.kk7.ch (approximate)

The only update since last month is that the club has submitted our official response.

Essentially we’ve said we’re not fundamentally opposed and already working with the Proposer, but at the same time that without satisfactory implementation of these mitigation strategies the impact of this proposed TDA is considered by the DHPC to be grossly disproportionate.

For the full background and response see the forum post https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=3785,

sections.title: Professor of the Dark Arts Required (no that’s not XC Flying!)
sections.content:
Tam reports

At the AGM next December, Dave is going to stand down as Treasurer, a post he has held for 6 years.  Treasurer is clearly a key post in the club, and required by our constitution.  We will therefore need someone to step forward and fill Dave’s boots. You do not need to be a qualified accountant, just be organised and willing to put something back into the club.  Membership of the cmte does not need to be an onerous task, and all manner of things get discussed at meetings, which can if necessary be attended remotely, Committee meetings are held 5 times a year, plus the AGM.  We are advertising this post early, as there would be an opportunity to shadow Dave for the rest of the year, so that you are already familiar with exactly what’s required in the role before you actually take over in December. If anyone would like a chat to find out a bit more about the role and how it all works you can contact  Tam (chairman@dhpc.org.uk) or Dave (treasurer@dhpc.org.uk) for an initial chat.

sections.title: It’s all downhill from here.. (or how I did 100k in my first XC)
sections.content:
Simon Hulme reports

 

It all started with a little bit of chatter on one of the various DHPC chat groups.   Which one I can’t remember but RASP was looking good for central England and a moderate westerly wind was favouring Long Mynd.  I knew the area along the Welsh Borders from my days flying Hawks out of RAF Valley in Anglesey as the Clee Hills were a common low level entry point.  That said it’s all a bit different dangling under a teacloth and spending half your time going round in circles.

 

The rest of the evening was spent having a good look at the airspace and weather as well as working out how to load NOTAMs to my Oudie N.  I planned what I thought were an optimistic 60km task and an absolute stretch target of 100km and retired for the night with the alarm set for 0500.  Pete Logan looked like he was up for the trip but was still not home from work and not committing to the journey until he’d had a chance to review the weather.

 

0600 departure from Bedale, 0700 collect Pete Logan from Baildon, 0950 arrive in Church Stretton to rendezvous with the rest of the team and pool cars for the drive up to the ‘Mynd’ – apparently all the cool kids skip the ‘Long.’  Skoda Superb full to the gunnels I drove up to the take-off spot filled with just a little bit of apprehension.  Chief Coach Pete gave me a good chat on the weather and the potential issues with damp ground and thermals due to the heavy rain over the preceding days as well as a brief on the site.  The online guides for the Mynd are great but I was still glad to see a nice large smoothly grassed launch area and no gliders out on the adjacent take off.  My stretch task was trumped by the 120ish goal that had been agreed by the big boys in a group I wasn’t actually a member of – doh!

 

Being a newb I am fully aware that I should be doing more ground handling – no excuses but, when there’s flying to be had I’d still rather take it.  However, I was beginning to regret that as I sat on launch watching Pete, Rahul and the rest of the gang disappear over the back of the hill locked into a solid looking thermal – that was the last I’d see of them until after landing!  To be fair I wasn’t even strapped into the harness when the drama began on the ground – wing laid out downwind, harness connected all ready to bunch the wing and strap in, sadly as I shouldered the harness the wind caught the downwind tip and 30 seconds of carnage ensued until Pete Logan managed to lend his bulk to pin down the flapping mess.  Off to the sin bin at the back of the launch for me to sort the spaghetti!  Pete did lend a hand thankfully but was off as soon as the lines were sorted to join the fun.  Composure regained. I was standing on the front face of launch in a slightly intimidating wind watching someone have a bit of a drama with his wing.  Once he’d given up I was off – first time success! And relax …..time to focus on that first thermal, not too busy, first gaggle 20 minutes ahead and a glorious looking sky.  10 minutes later I was locked into the first solid thermal and committed over the back. 1100m and 7km from launch time to focus on what lay ahead.  A lovely sky full of well-developed cumulus and a scattering of wings on the horizon as well as some just about intelligible chatter from the gaggle.

Climbout from the Long Mynd. Photo credit Jon Oliver.

 

15km of tail wind was less than I was expecting at height but I can’t really complain, on the trackline for goal and the ridgeline from Callow Hill stretching out to the NE on the nose, Clee Hills beyond that.  The next thermal triggered off the ridgeline – boom, well not really, the climbs weren’t overly strong but I was back to 1100m and had a lovely cloud street ahead.  I couldn’t really see any cycle to the clouds building and decaying but there was definitely lift underneath and for the next 40 minutes I took a series of small climbs and a generally lifty line and didn’t drop much below 1000m.  At some point under this cloud street I was joined by another pilot who was making quicker progress than me but that was the only company I had had for over an hour.   Now time for a good glide and to find the next thermal.  Bottomed out at about 400m AGL approaching Callow Hill and there we go – another straight to 1400m.  No more lifty lines now though, just more distinct climbs and glides but still plenty of cloud on the nose to head for.  Looking over my shoulder there was clearly some overdevelopment happening behind me.

 

By this stage I was in a bit of a time warp, 2 hours had passed,, I’d caught up with a few other wings and there was plenty of traffic to focus on with a series of sailplanes hugging cloud base, twin prop aircraft and a couple of hangies.  I joined a thermal with a sailplane  but was struggling to keep track in my tight little turns as he seemed to be barely turning around the outside..  Topped out over Droitwich at 1600m and off on glide again, no cloud to aim for but a small distinct town on the nose and they seemed to be working as triggers.  Sure enough a sniff of a thermal at 600m AGL and managed to take this one all the way back up to 1600m.

 

2 hours and 40 minutes in and it was starting to show.  I wasn’t cold – hadn’t really managed to get to cloud base for most of the day.  Despite hearing chatter about the climbs consolidating closer to cloud base I was struggling to keep hold and so was setting off down route as the climbs weakened.  I was struggling to manipulate my Oudie screen with the winter gloves so was having to take off a glove carefully to change scales with the Birmingham airspace on the nose.   I definitely don’t have a preference as to which way I thermal but by now I certainly did have a sore neck and it was starting to become a distraction – note to self, I must set a more upright seating position before I’m out next.

 

A couple of more decent climbs with no need for low saves, a bit of a panic when my instrument was warning of airspace on the nose that I thought I was below -  I was but that was the first time I’d been near airspace on a paraglider. I was now just south of Stratford on Avon and genuinely believed the goal was a certain thing! It was around this time that I caught up with a few higher performance wings including as it turns out Helen Grant, who flew the best distance of the day by a good margin.  

 

So this is where I should have taken note …. The group I was now in the vicinity of were taking things much steadier.  I could see a really good cloud on the nose, it turned out to be over Banbury and well out of reach, and what I thought were a bunch of really good triggers both terrain and manmade, but nothing was really giving the climbs that I had seen earlier in the day.  I should have switched modes – so easy to see in hindsight! I was turning in zeros but the wind was dropping at height and it felt like I was going nowhere.  My flying was a bit less precise, roll and pitch control was more erratic – I could have done with a double espresso!  I was south abeam Edgehill Gliding Club, which was busy but 1000m below.

 

My Oudie chirped into life to tell me I was on final glide – sucker! I’d had 5 minutes drifting along in zeros and as it turns out if I’d have made a bit of effort to stay in that thermal, I’d have connected with the monster climb over goal but I turned and set up my best glide for goal.  A couple of nice villages on the nose, surely, they’d trigger something.  Dawning realisation I’d stolen defeat from the jaws of victory.  Time to focus on finding a low save and a safe landing; terrain was rising on the nose but still height for a pass over the village of North Newington.  Nothing happening, left base land 1 minute later.  

 

Safe and sound on the ground but completely deflated! Next thing the radio chirps into life – it was Pete Logan.  ‘Simon, if you pack up quickly I’ve got us a lift to Banbury station.’  I hadn’t seen Pete for over 4 hours, but we landed 20 minutes apart in adjacent fields having taken quite different lines.  Maybe it wasn’t such a bad day after all.  A very kind chap and fellow paraglider had seen Pete land and headed straight over.   A quick trip to Banbury station where we met Dougie Swanson Low, the only person to make the Banbury goal, and 5 minutes later we were on the train to Birmingham New Street – bonza!  Time for a quick celebratory beer.   Birmingham, Wolverhampton, Shrewsbury and Church Stretton with great connections and Dougie’s car still parked at the station.  Now for the 4-hour drive home to Bedale.

 

What an adventure!  Home at midnight after an 18 hour day - safe to say I slept well.  I was simultaneously completely gutted not to make goal but also chuffed with the day out and making over 100km the first time I’d left the hill in the UK.  I’d read lots and been lucky enough to get strong thermalling flights done in Annecy and Morocco.  I was completely in the zone when airborne and not at all daunted by setting off from the Mynd on my own.  I’m sure my professional background, flying fighters in the RAF has played a massive part in shaping my mentality.   But I have to say we have a great club and a group of coaches, led by Pete Logan, and a bunch of experienced pilots always willing to help develop the newbies to the sport.  I sat at the last annual club presentations admiring what James Maxwell and Jordan Whittaker had both achieved the preceding year with their epic XC achievements – I’m sure they too have benefitted from the wealth of knowledge and support from within the DHPC.

I’d say there was a massive amount of luck in my flight, but as they say: ‘you have to be in it to win it.’  Pete Logan, Richard and Jacob Butterworth, Rahul, Andy Beevers, John Oliver, Helen Grant, Eliot Brown, Richard Carter and Scot O’Neill also joined the raiding party to conquer the Mynd and I’m sure all were chuffed they’d made the effort..

 

PS I didn’t even make it off the hill from Semer Water the following week and I bombed on my next XC in Algo after 17km also thinking goal at 25 km was a certainty ☹️

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

It’s that time of year again: Spring

As usual, please pay attention to your currency and the already encountered rather spicy spring thermal conditions. If you need a reminder of whats at stake, see below incident report. If you haven’t flown for a while, do some ground handling to familiarize yourself again with your kit, and take it easy.

And then at the end of that amazing flight, please please please, avoid fields with pregnant sheep and/or lambs !

Serious accident at Whitestones.

(Copied with permission from the pilot involved)

subsections.title: Victim of Spring Thermal - Whitestones 20 Mar 26
subsections.content:
Friday 20 March. After about an hour and a half of increasingly thermic flying at Whitestones setting up to land in usual place adjacent to carpark. Wind socks showed nil wind so prepared for a fast landing. I took a wrap on the brakes but failed to achieve this on the left side due to unfamiliarity with a new pair of gloves. Almost simultaneously with the failed wrap I saw the windsock flick violently and I immediately suffered from an asymmetric on the right. I was aware of being spun. Next thing I remember was a voice (visiting pilot, Lizzy) telling me not to move. I had been unconscious briefly but was able to establish all my bits were working before exiting my harness and wobbling around the landing area for a bit. Initially I had absolutely no idea of where I was, how I got there or who Lizzy was even though I had given her a site brief and chatted with her on the hill an hour earlier. Initially I had no recall of flying let alone the incident. As my memory returned I deduced the following. I had been ambushed by a short sharp thermal. The effect was probably exacerbated by having a wrap on the right brake.

The main lesson (re-lesson)is that Whitestones can be extremely thermic, particularly at this time of the year. I should have made more allowance for the conditions and probably not attempted to land in a fairly restricted area given the massive available area. Also, I'm not particularly current given the last couple of seasons have been limited by an ongoing back problem. Moreover, think I was probably distracted and faffing with my left hand glove.

Injuries! I feel quite lucky. Feeling battered with some colourful bruising but nothing broken. Sadly my back recovery programme is in jeopardy but I do have a frequent flyer pass with the Penrith Physio department.

Martin Baxter reports

On 28 Feb, whilst I was ‘enjoying’ myself at the BHPA AGM, a number of you flew at Windbank.  Afterwards Jake commented on the forum that it was quite crosswind and borderline for those of us who may be a little uncurrent.  Luckily there were no incidents, perhaps because it was a fairly laminar day with little in the way of thermic activity.  I wonder how many pilots that day considered the implications of flying in a crosswind, or just flew because others were staying up.

I recently returned from a trip to Morocco where loads of pilots were scratching in significant crosswinds.  It scared the shit out of me!  I couldn’t help wondering how many novices were learning false lessons.  Following Chris Williams’ accident 3 years ago in seemingly benign conditions, I’m now extra cautious when soaring with a crosswind.  If you haven’t watched the video of his analysis of the accident, then I suggest that you do; and be particularly wary now that the thermals have started kicking in.

sections.title: Photos
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

I don’t have instagram so I’m going to inflict this stuff on you lot.

Andrew and Clive with some CSC reprobates at the reserve zipline event near Keswick. Seriously, sign up next year, it’s well worth doing.

A clean throw…

Me on the way to Banbury from the Mynd. Lovely unstable conditions but not punchy. Read about this day in Simon’s article above.


Another from Jon Oliver.

A gentle floaty day at Stags ending with a fly down to the Green Dragon. Tam puts in a big flare.

Toots nails the sunset shot.
